data.title: This AI-Based Defense Company Secured A US Government Contract – So, Why Did The Stock Reverse Pre-Market Gains?
data.description:
Key points:
  • Safe Pro will deliver advanced AI solutions for defense, security, and situational awareness applications.
  • Development and low-rate initial production of Safe Pro’s systems were funded by Ondas and Unusual Machines.
  • The company recently partnered with Lantronix to develop chipsets for Qualcomm-based drone and autonomous vehicle platforms.

Shares of Safe Pro Group (SPAI) reversed pre-market gains to trade 6% lower on Friday, after the company said it secured a $1 million subcontract to supply AI processing systems to the U.S. Government through a prime contractor.

According to the contract, Safe Pro will deliver advanced artificial intelligence solutions for defense, security, and situational awareness applications.

Development and low-rate initial production of the systems were funded by U.S.-based firms, including Ondas (ONDS) and Unusual Machines (UMAC).

How Did Stocktwits Users React?

Retail sentiment on Stocktwits turned ‘bullish’ from ‘neutral,’ but chatter was mixed.

Following the contract announcement, one user called the news “bullish” and said they were long on the stock, while another chose to remain on the sidelines, arguing that the contract size was too small to be meaningful.

https://stocktwits.com/DrPiz/message/645426697

https://stocktwits.com/Tradebub/message/645427119

Partnerships With Lantronix And Ondas In Last Two Months

The company recently signed a series of partnerships. Last month, Safe Pro signed a master services agreement and an MoU with Lantronix (LTRX) to jointly develop embeddable chipsets for Qualcomm-based drone and autonomous vehicle platforms.

In December, Safe Pro and Ondas completed a Middle East pilot program using AI to support demining efforts, validating Safe Pro Object Threat Detection’s (SPOTD) ability to detect explosive threats in aerial imagery. The company had also filed a patent application for its AI-powered computer vision technology.

Earlier that month, the company formed SPAI Ventures subsidiary to focus on strategic investments, including collaborations with Ukrainian and other international technology developers to support the growth of its SPOTD AI image analysis platform and ballistic protection products.

Year-to-date, the stock has shed around 6%.

data.title: OpenAI developing AI devices including smart speaker, The Information reports
data.description:
OpenAI has more than 200 people working on a family of AI-powered devices that will include a smart speaker and possibly smart glasses and a smart lamp, the Information reported on Friday, citing a person with knowledge of the plans.

The smart speaker, the first device OpenAI will launch, is likely to be priced between $200 and $300, the report said, citing two people with knowledge of it. The speaker, which, however, would not ship until February 2027 at earliest, will have a camera, enabling it to take in information about its users and their surroundings, the report said.

The smart glasses likely would not be ready for mass production until 2028, the report said.

OpenAI did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

The ChatGPT owner entered the hardware industry with the $6.5 billion acquisition of former Apple designer Jony Ive's startup io Products, aiming to capitalize on the growing demand for physical AI and augmented reality.

Facebook-parent Meta has seen strong success with its Ray-Ban smart glasses, which allows users to record and take pictures, and stream content through tiny cameras. Apple and Google are also reportedly working on their own smart glasses.

data.title: Here's what Paramount would have to do for Netflix to walk away from its Warner Bros. deal
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

A MoffettNathanson analyst says that if Paramount pushes its price above $32 per share, Netflix may be too disciplined to continue bidding on something that is 'not a must have'

Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos testified before a Senate committee earlier this month about his company's effort to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery. With the Warner Bros. board reopening talks with rival Paramount, questions have arisen about how far Netflix might be willing to go to win.

For months, Netflix has been in the driver's seat of an industry-shaking effort to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery. But that company's decision earlier this week to reopen talks with rival Paramount Skydance has opened the door for a scenario in which Netflix could walk away.

The Warner Bros. (WBD) board of directors on Tuesday decided to grant Paramount (PSKY) a seven-day window to present its best and final offer on a promise that Paramount would raise its bid to at least $31 per share from $30 per share.

But Paramount has suggested it may not stop there. How high it is willing to go may dictate how far a typically "disciplined" Netflix (NFLX) might go to stay in the game, according to Robert Fishman, a media analyst at MoffettNathanson Research.

He argues that the acquisition of Warner Bros.' studio and streaming divisions is a "nice to have" but not a "must have" for Netflix, so a Paramount bid above $32 a share may be enough to take Netflix out of the running.

"While we see the longer-term benefits of owning Warner Bros., HBO and HBO Max, we expect [Netflix] to walk away from the deal following a disciplined approach if [Paramount] pushes its bid well beyond $32 per share," he wrote in a note to clients.

And it may take that level of aggressiveness, Fishman says, if Paramount really wants to push its bid over the line.

"We think it will be difficult for [Paramount] to win the bidding war for [Warner Bros.] if it decides to take a less aggressive approach during this waiver period, giving [Netflix] the opportunity to match at a more modest increase from its current bid," he wrote.

Paramount has already offered $108 billion to acquire all of Warner Bros. Discover, versus Netflix's bid of $82.7 billion, which amounts to $27.75 per share, for just the streaming and studio divisions. That would leave Warner Bros.' declining television unit, which includes channels like CNN, Discovery and the Cartoon Network, to be spun off into a separate company.

The Warner Bros. board decided in early December that it preferred Netflix's bid, saying the Paramount bid undervalued its television assets and raising questions about that company's financing.

Since then, Paramount has launched a hostile-takeover effort, trying to convince shareholders to vote against the board's recommendation. The Warner Bros. board has repeatedly urged shareholders to stick with Netflix, and up until now, Paramount had gained little traction, as it has so far declined to raise its price.

But last week, the ground shifted slightly after Paramount offered several deal "enhancements" to convince shareholders to walk away from the Netflix deal.

Among them was an additional 25-cent-per-share "ticking fee" for every quarter Paramount's bid failed to close past Dec. 31, 2026, which could amount to an additional $650 million for each quarter. Paramount also agreed to cover the cost of Netflix's $2.8 billion termination fee if Warner Bros. walks away from the offer. Paramount also said it would wipe out a $1.5 billion financing fee Warner Bros. shareholders would have to pay as part of an exchange of debt that would accompany a Paramount deal.

Since then, activist investor Ancora Holdings disclosed it has taken a substantial stake in Warner Bros. and said it would vote to reject the Netflix proposal.

Many in Hollywood have expressed deep concerns about a Netflix takeover, saying they worry it would drive the final nail into the coffin of the movie theater business. Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos has long said he views the theatrical release model as outdated.

"Titanic" director James Cameron decried the deal in a letter to Congress, saying he believes it would kill the movie theater business.

Netflix has insisted that it has no plans to make changes to Warner Bros.' theatrical release model and that it intends to run the studio much the way it currently is done, separately from its streaming business.

Paramount has argued that its bid has the best chance of passing muster with regulators, given the close relationship between its CEO David Ellison and his father, Oracle (ORCL) co-founder Larry Ellison, with President Donald Trump. At one point, Paramount's offer was partly backed by Trump's son-in-law, Jared Kushner, although he later stepped away from the deal.

The Trump administration and regulators in Europe have signaled that they intend to closely scrutinize any deal.

Netflix has argued that Paramount is misleading investors by saying its proposal would have an easier path to regulatory approval and that the amount of debt its offer would entail would require extremely deep cost cuts that would damage the entertainment industry.

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US Supreme Court rejects Trump's global tariffs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump invoked law meant for emergencies to impose tariffs
  • Lower courts ruled Trump acted unlawfully in doing so
  • Constitution gives Congress the authority to issue tariffs

By Andrew Chung

The U.S. Supreme Court struck down on Friday President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs that he pursued under a law meant for use in national emergencies, rejecting one of his most contentious assertions of his authority in a ruling with major implications for the global economy.

Trump has leveraged tariffs - taxes on imported goods - as a key economic and foreign policy tool.

They have been central to a global trade war that Trump initiated after he began his second term as president, one that has alienated trading partners, affected financial markets and caused global economic uncertainty.

Trump's tariffs were forecast to generate over the next decade trillions of dollars in revenue for the United States, which possesses the world's largest economy.

Trump's administration has not provided tariffs collection data since December 14. But Penn-Wharton Budget Model economists estimated on Friday that the amount collected in Trump's tariffs based on the International Emergency Economic Powers Act stood at more than $175 billion. And that amount likely would need to be refunded with a Supreme Court ruling against the IEEPA-based tariffs.

The U.S. Constitution grants Congress, not the president, the authority to issue taxes and tariffs. But Trump instead turned to a statutory authority by invoking IEEPA to impose the tariffs on nearly every U.S. trading partner without the approval of Congress. Trump has imposed some additional tariffs under other laws that are not at issue in this case. Based on government data from October to mid-December, those represent about third of the revenue from Trump-imposed tariffs.

IEEPA lets a president regulate commerce in a national emergency. Trump became the first president to use IEEPA to impose tariffs, one of the many ways he has aggressively pushed the boundaries of executive authority since he returned to office in areas as varied as his crackdown on immigration, the firing of federal agency officials, domestic military deployments and military operations overseas.

Trump described the tariffs as vital for U.S. economic security, predicting that the country would be defenseless and ruined without them. Trump in November told reporters that without his tariffs "the rest of the world would laugh at us because they've used tariffs against us for years and took advantage of us." Trump said the United States was abused by other countries including China, the second-largest economy.

After the Supreme Court heard arguments in the case in November, Trump said he would consider alternatives if it ruled against him on tariffs, telling reporters that "we'll have to develop a 'game two' plan."

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and other administration officials said the United States would invoke other legal justifications to retain as many of Trump's tariffs as possible. Among others, these include a statutory provision that permits tariffs on imported goods that threaten U.S. national security and another that allows retaliatory actions including tariffs against trading partners that the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative determines have used unfair trade practices against American exporters.

None of these alternatives offered the flexibility and blunt-force dynamics that IEEPA provided Trump, and may not be able to replicate the full scope of his tariffs in a timely fashion.

Trump's ability to impose tariffs instantaneously on any trading partner's goods under the aegis of some form of declared national emergency raised his leverage over other countries. It brought world leaders scrambling to Washington to secure trade deals that often included pledges of billions of dollars in investments or other offers of enhanced market access for U.S. companies.

But Trump's use of tariffs as a cudgel in U.S. foreign policy has succeeded in antagonizing numerous countries, including those long considered among the closest U.S. allies.

IEEPA historically had been used for imposing sanctions on enemies or freezing their assets, not to impose tariffs. The law does not specifically mention the word tariffs. Trump's Justice Department had argued that IEEPA allows tariffs by authorizing the president to "regulate" imports to address emergencies.

The Congressional Budget Office has estimated that if all current tariffs stay in place, including the IEEPA-based duties, they would generate about $300 billion annually over the next decade.

Total U.S. net customs duty receipts reached a record $195 billion in fiscal 2025, which ended on September 30, according to U.S. Treasury Department data.

On April 2 on a date Trump labeled "Liberation Day," the president announced what he called "reciprocal" tariffs on goods imported from most U.S. trading partners, invoking IEEPA to address what he called a national emergency related to U.S. trade deficits, though the United States already had run trade deficits for decades.

In February and March of 2025, Trump invoked IEEPA to impose tariffs on China, Canada and Mexico, citing the trafficking of the often-abused painkiller fentanyl and illicit drugs into the United States as a national emergency.

Trump has wielded his tariffs to extract concessions and renegotiate trade deals, and as a weapon to punish countries that draw his ire on non-trade political matters. These have ranged from Brazil's prosecution of former president Jair Bolsonaro, India's purchases of Russian oil that help fund Russia's war in Ukraine, and an anti-tariffs ad by Canada's Ontario province.

IEEPA was passed by Congress and signed by Democratic President Jimmy Carter. In passing the measure, Congress placed additional limits on the president's authority compared to a predecessor law.

The cases on tariffs before the justices involved three lawsuits.

The Washington-based U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit sided with five small businesses that import goods in one challenge, and the states of Arizona, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Illinois, Maine, Minnesota, Nevada, New Mexico, New York, Oregon and Vermont in another.

Separately, a Washington-based federal judge sided with a family-owned toy company called Learning Resources.

data.title: What charge might King Charles' brother face after his arrest?
data.description:
King Charles' younger brother Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor was arrested on suspicion of misconduct in public office this week, over allegations that he sent confidential British government documents to disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein.

Thames Valley Police, whose detectives questioned the former prince all day on Thursday, have said they are looking into allegations that Mountbatten-Windsor had passed documents to the late convicted U.S. sex offender while working as a trade envoy.

Among files relating to Epstein released by the U.S., some suggest that Mountbatten-Windsor forwarded to Epstein in 2010 reports about potential business opportunities in places he had visited as the Special Representative for Trade and Investment.

The former prince, who turned 66 on Thursday, has always denied any wrongdoing in relation to Epstein. He was released from custody late on Thursday while the investigation continues.

Here are details about the potential charge he faces:

WHAT IS MISCONDUCT IN PUBLIC OFFICE?

The offence of misconduct in public office is a 'common law' offence, meaning it is not covered by written legislation but has been developed over centuries by judges' rulings.

A conviction for misconduct in public office carries a maximum sentence of life imprisonment. Police have previously said the offence involves "particular complexities" that require a careful assessment, without elaborating further.

Britain's top prosecutor Stephen Parkinson said this month that the law on misconduct in public office is "quite clear".

However, the offence has largely been used against lower-ranking police and prison staff, according to research published last year by campaign group Spotlight on Corruption, which said prosecutors might struggle with complex offences.

In one case, a former policeman was handed a 10-month jail sentence in 2013 for selling a story to The Sun newspaper and trying to sell another, about the child of celebrities, to the now-defunct News of the World.

The independent Law Commission in 2020 recommended replacing the offence of misconduct in public office and a law is currently going through parliament to do so, though it would not have retrospective effect.

WHAT DO PROSECUTORS HAVE TO PROVE?

Tom Frost, a law lecturer at Loughborough University, said misconduct in public office "remains a difficult offence to prove in relation to senior executives or senior political figures".

Firstly, prosecutors have to show that an individual was in a public office and "acting as, not simply whilst, a public official" at the time of an alleged offence.

As Mountbatten-Windsor's trade envoy role was unpaid - though he received free travel and accommodation - the case for arguing he was acting as a public official might be less clear, though guidance from the Crown Prosecution Service says remuneration is "not determinative".

Prosecutors also need to prove that a defendant wilfully misconducted themselves "to such a degree as to amount to an abuse of the public's trust".

The Court of Appeal said in 2003 that the bar is a high one and that the conduct had to be "not merely negligent but ... an affront to the standing of the public office held".

Misconduct in public office also requires that the conduct had no reasonable excuse or justification.

WHAT HAPPENS NEXT?

Given the complexity of misconduct in public office, it is likely that police and prosecutors will take several weeks or even months to make a decision on whether to bring charges.

Mountbatten-Windsor, who has always denied any wrongdoing in relation to Epstein but has not spoken publicly since the release of further files, might well face further questioning.

In the event that he is charged, the former prince would have to attend a magistrates' court for his first appearance.

Misconduct in public office can only be tried by a judge and jury, meaning Andrew's case would be sent to the Crown Court, which handles the most serious criminal cases, most likely the famous Old Bailey in central London.

There are long-standing delays in the British criminal courts, with some trials currently being listed as far ahead as 2030, though any trial of the king's brother would most likely be heard sooner.

data.title: US Business Activity Slows to 10-Month Low
data.description:
The S&P Global US Composite PMI fell to 52.3 in February 2026 from 53.0 in January, signaling the slowest pace of private-sector expansion since April 2025, according to preliminary data.

Growth moderated across both sectors, with manufacturing and services activity easing to seven- and ten-month lows, respectively.

New orders also softened, while export demand declined at one of the sharpest rates in the past year.

Employment increased only marginally for a third straight month, marking the weakest job growth since last April.

On the pricing side, input cost inflation edged higher, often linked to tariffs and rising labor expenses, while output prices climbed at the fastest pace since August. Despite the slowdown, business confidence strengthened, with year-ahead output expectations rising to a 13-month high.

data.title: OLIN Corp SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Olin Corporation, a leading manufacturer in the chemical and ammunition industries, has released its 2025 Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. This summary highlights the key aspects of Olin's performance and strategic direction as outlined in the report.

Financial Highlights

  • Sales: $6,780.8 million, an increase of $240.7 million or 4% from 2024, driven by higher volumes across segments.
  • Gross Margin: $501.5 million, decreased by $236.0 million from 2024, primarily due to lower pricing and higher raw material costs.
  • Operating Income: $5.3 million, a significant decrease from $296.5 million in 2024, impacted by increased operating expenses and litigation charges.
  • Net (Loss) Income Attributable to Olin Corporation: $(100.5) million, compared to net income of $108.6 million in 2024, reflecting lower segment results and increased costs.
  • Net (Loss) Income Per Common Share - Basic: $(0.88), compared to $0.92 in 2024, reflecting the overall decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The Chlor Alkali Products and Vinyls segment accounted for 54% of 2025 sales, the Epoxy segment represented 20%, and the Winchester segment contributed 26%.
  • Geographical Performance: Approximately 32% of Olin's 2025 sales were generated outside of the U.S., with 31% from Chlor Alkali Products and Vinyls, 59% from Epoxy, and 13% from Winchester.
  • Sales Units: The Winchester segment experienced decreased commercial ammunition sales volumes, while military project revenue increased.
  • New Production Launches: The joint venture Hidrogenii, LLC, launched in 2024, began operations of a 15-ton-per-day hydrogen liquefaction plant in St. Gabriel, LA, in the second quarter of 2025.
  • Future Outlook: In 2026, Olin expects higher operating results from its Chemicals businesses due to the absence of a non-recurring charge, despite anticipated higher maintenance and raw material costs. Winchester's results are expected to improve due to normalized inventories at retail customers.
  • Operational Challenges: The Chlor Alkali Products and Vinyls segment faced lower pricing and higher raw material and operating costs, while the Epoxy segment dealt with higher operating costs and market saturation from subsidized Asian competition.
  • Strategic Developments: Olin ended its joint venture with Mitsui, Blue Water Alliance, to focus on longer-term structural opportunities in EDC participation. A new commercial arrangement with Braskem was announced for EDC supply.
  • Environmental and Safety Initiatives: Olin is committed to reducing its environmental impact through technology and innovation, aiming to lower its carbon footprint and resource consumption.
  • Human Capital: Olin emphasizes employee engagement and development, with a focus on safety, professional growth, and community involvement, as demonstrated by over 56,000 volunteer hours in 2025.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: Olin Corporation executed several strategic initiatives in 2025, including the acquisition of AMMO, Inc.'s small caliber ammunition manufacturing assets for $55.8 million, which was financed with cash on hand. The company also announced the termination of its joint venture, Blue Water Alliance, with Mitsui & Co., Ltd., to focus on longer-term structural opportunities in the EDC market. Additionally, Olin entered into a commercial arrangement with Braskem to supply EDC, aligning with Braskem's transformation of its chlor alkali and vinyl assets in Brazil.
  • Capital Management: In 2025, Olin Corporation repurchased and retired 2.2 million shares of common stock at a total value of $50.5 million, with $1.9 billion remaining under its 2022 and 2024 Repurchase Authorization programs. The company issued $600 million of 6.625% senior notes due 2033 and entered into a new $1,850 million senior credit facility, increasing its borrowing limit by $300 million and extending the maturity date to 2030. Olin also refinanced its existing credit facilities and redeemed $608.6 million of senior notes. Dividends per common share were maintained at $0.80, with total dividends paid amounting to $91.6 million.
  • Future Outlook: For 2026, Olin anticipates higher operating results in the first quarter compared to the fourth quarter of 2025, despite expected higher maintenance and raw material costs. The company plans to pay approximately $185 million to Shintech related to a litigation matter and expects capital spending to be around $200 million. Olin forecasts its effective tax rate to be between 20% and 30% and expects to receive refunds related to the clean hydrogen production tax credit. The company also plans to maintain its focus on liquidity and capital management, with environmental expenses projected to range between $25 million and $35 million.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market Risks: The company is sensitive to global economic conditions, which can lead to reduced demand for its products. Cyclical pricing pressure in the chemical and ammunition industries can also affect profitability.
  • Operational Risks: The company's operating model emphasizes ECU margins over sales volume, which may not always be successful. Cost control is critical, and rising raw material, utility, and transportation costs could reduce profitability. Production hazards, such as equipment failure or natural disasters, pose significant risks.
  • Regulatory Risks: Changes in environmental regulations, such as the EPA's ban on asbestos, could increase production costs or lead to capacity closures. Compliance with international regulations, such as REACH in Europe, may also increase operational costs.
  • Emerging Risks: The company faces cybersecurity threats that could disrupt operations. Climate-related events, particularly in the U.S. Gulf Coast, pose physical risks to facilities.
  • Financial Risks: High levels of indebtedness could limit financial flexibility, and adverse credit market conditions may affect the company's ability to refinance debt.

SEC Filing: OLIN Corp [ OLN ] - 10-K - Feb. 20, 2026

data.title: COCA COLA CO SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
The Coca-Cola Company, a global leader in the beverage industry, has released its 2025 Form 10-K report, detailing significant financial growth, strategic initiatives, and operational highlights. The report underscores the company's commitment to innovation, sustainability, and adapting to evolving consumer preferences in a highly competitive market.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Operating Revenues: $47,941 million, an increase of $880 million or 2% from 2024, driven by favorable pricing initiatives and concentrate sales volume growth.
  • Gross Profit Margin: 61.6%, up from 61.1% in 2024, primarily due to favorable pricing initiatives and refranchising impacts.
  • Operating Income: $13,762 million, an increase of $3,770 million or 38% from 2024, primarily driven by lower other operating charges and favorable pricing initiatives.
  • Interest Income: $786 million, a decrease of $202 million or 20% from 2024, due to lower average investment balances.
  • Interest Expense: $1,654 million, a slight decrease of $2 million from 2024, due to lower average short-term debt balances.
  • Equity Income (Loss) — Net: $2,031 million, an increase of $261 million or 15% from 2024, reflecting more favorable operating results from equity method investees.
  • Other Income (Loss) — Net: $1,073 million, including a gain of $1,952 million from the sale of an equity method investee.
  • Income Taxes: $2,861 million, with an effective tax rate of 17.9%, reflecting tax incentives and benefits from equity method investments.
  • Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities: $7,408 million, an increase of $603 million or 9% from 2024, driven by strong cash operating results and lower tax payments.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The Coca-Cola Company operates in two main lines of business: concentrate operations and finished product operations. Concentrate operations accounted for 59% of total net operating revenues, while finished product operations contributed 41%.
  • Geographical Performance: The company's operations are divided into several geographic segments: EMEA, Latin America, North America, Asia Pacific, and Bottling Investments. In 2025, unit case volume in the United States represented 16% of the worldwide volume, with Mexico, China, Brazil, and India being the largest markets outside the U.S., collectively accounting for 33% of worldwide unit case volume.
  • Sales Units: The Coca-Cola system sold 33.8 billion unit cases in 2025, with sparkling soft drinks representing 69% of worldwide unit case volume. Trademark Coca-Cola accounted for 47% of worldwide unit case volume.
  • New Product Launches: The company has expanded its portfolio to include alcohol ready-to-drink beverages in numerous markets outside the United States, focusing on pre-mixed cocktails, flavored alcohol beverages, and hard seltzers.
  • New Production Launches: Effective January 1, 2025, the company streamlined its operations by sunsetting the Global Ventures operating segment, integrating Costa, innocent, and doğadan into the EMEA segment, and reallocating Costa's ready-to-drink business and Monster-related fees to applicable geographic segments.
  • Future Outlook: The company is focused on shaping a portfolio of loved brands, transforming its marketing and innovation agenda, optimizing the Coca-Cola ecosystem, building talent and capabilities, and enhancing its license to win. It aims to address challenges such as obesity concerns, evolving consumer preferences, and competition in the digital marketplace.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: The Coca-Cola Company is focused on optimizing its operations through strategic refranchising of bottling operations in various regions, including India, Bangladesh, and the Philippines. The company is also investing in its portfolio of brands and capabilities, with a focus on innovation and sustainability to meet evolving consumer preferences. Additionally, the company is enhancing its digital marketplace presence to maintain competitiveness and brand loyalty.
  • Capital Management: The company maintains a prudent debt level, using debt financing to lower overall cost of capital and increase return on equity. In 2025, the company issued $4,980 million in debt and made payments of $4,967 million. The company also repurchased 9.4 million shares of common stock for $746 million under the 2019 share repurchase plan. Dividends paid in 2025 totaled $8,779 million, with a subsequent increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.53 per share. The company continues to manage its capital allocation priorities, including supporting business operations, growing dividends, and repurchasing shares.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead, Coca-Cola plans to continue its strategic initiatives by investing approximately $2.2 billion in capital expenditures in 2026. The company also expects to repurchase shares to offset dilution from employee stock-based compensation plans. Additionally, the company anticipates a favorable impact from foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations on its 2026 net operating revenues. The effective tax rate for 2026 is expected to be approximately 20.9%, excluding potential impacts from ongoing tax litigation with the IRS.

Challenges and Risks

  • Economic and Geopolitical Risks: Unfavorable global economic conditions, geopolitical instability, and international conflicts could negatively impact financial results. These factors may lead to supply chain disruptions, increased costs, and reduced consumer demand.
  • Competition: Increased competition in the beverage industry could lead to price reductions and increased marketing costs, affecting profitability.
  • Consumer Preferences: Evolving consumer preferences towards health and wellness, as well as environmental concerns, may reduce demand for certain products.
  • Supply Chain Disruptions: Disruptions in the supply chain, including increased costs of raw materials and energy, could adversely affect operations.
  • Regulatory Risks: Changes in tax laws, increased indirect taxes, and new regulations on packaging and ingredients could increase costs and reduce demand.
  • Cybersecurity and Data Privacy: The company is exposed to risks related to cybersecurity incidents and data privacy, which could disrupt operations and damage reputation.
  • Environmental and Social Factors: Climate change, water scarcity, and sustainability expectations pose long-term risks to operations and reputation.
  • Management Strategies: Management is focused on addressing these challenges by enhancing digital capabilities, innovating product offerings, and strengthening relationships with bottling partners. The company is also investing in sustainability initiatives to meet evolving consumer and regulatory expectations.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates and interest rates, which could materially affect financial results. The company uses derivative financial instruments to mitigate some of these risks, but there is no assurance that these measures will be fully effective.

SEC Filing: COCA COLA CO [ KO ] - 10-K - Feb. 20, 2026

data.title: DEI Rules That Changed Corporate Boards Are Vanishing — WSJ
data.description:
By Theo Francis

Anti-diversity activists are going after DEI policies for corporate boards, but a new analysis finds that companies have largely abandoned those goals already.

S&P 500 companies are adding women and minority directors no faster than they did a decade ago, shortly before diversity, equity and inclusion policies became more common across the corporate world, according to a Wall Street Journal analysis of board data.

"It shows that all along, this was expendable," said Doug Chia, president of consulting firm Soundboard Governance.

Companies adopted such policies in droves over the past decade in the face of pressure from institutional investors and states such as California to diversify their largely white and male boardrooms. Goldman Sachs, until a year ago, also mandated that companies have diverse boards as a criteria for the bank to take them public.

But as the political winds have shifted, companies have swiftly retreated. This week, the Journal reported that Goldman Sachs plans to drop a policy of considering candidate demographics — including race, gender, ethnicity and sexual orientation — when recruiting its own new directors.

Other companies have already done so. Just a quarter of S&P 500 companies had a policy last year of considering gender, racial and ethnic diversity when adding directors, down from about half the year before, according to PeopleReturn, which collects and analyzes corporate human-capital data. Among the nearly three dozen companies making disclosures so far this year, that share has shrunk to 14%, PeopleReturn said.

Companies are also appointing fewer women and Black or Hispanic candidates to their boards. Nearly three quarters of last year's newly appointed directors were men at S&P 500 companies, and roughly four in five new appointments were white, PeopleReturn said.

That is on par with levels seen in 2016 at the same companies and a sharp decline in appointments of new Black, Latino and female directors, which peaked in 2021. That year, 40% of incoming directors were women and 45% weren't white.

Even where boards aren't intentionally de-emphasizing diversity, they could wind up seating more white men if they seek out experienced candidates to help in uncertain economic and political times, Chia said. "They're looking at an older generation of director, and typically that is not going to be women and minorities."

Simply paying less attention to diversity is also likely to leave boards looking more homogenous, said Atinuke Adediran, a Fordham University law professor who analyzes corporate diversity policies in a recent book, "Disclosureland."

"When you de-emphasize that, then you just call up your friends as you were doing before," she said.

Board seats turn over slowly — about one seat a year per board, PeopleReturn data show. As a result, the overall demographics of S&P 500 directors don't change much year to year: Around three quarters of directors have been white, and around two-thirds male, for the last several years.

The days of "stale, pale and male" boards — as the adage criticizing boards filled with mostly older, white men goes — aren't back just yet. A decade ago, 79% of directors were male and 85% were white at today's S&P 500 companies, PeopleReturn data show.

Still, at the current reduced pace for adding women, boards in the Russell 3000 wouldn't be half female until 2044, compensation and governance data firm Equilar projects. That is five years later than it projected last year and 11 years later than two years ago.

Behind the scenes, a range of factors are easing the pressure on companies to diversify their boards. Nasdaq's brief requirement that boards meet racial and gender targets, or explain why they haven't, was struck down by a federal appeals court in December 2024. California rules designed to foster a minimum number of female board members — as well as ethnic and racial minorities and lesbian, gay, bisexual or transgender directors — were struck down separately.

Early last year, Institutional Shareholder Services, the influential proxy-advisory firm, said it would no longer consider directors' gender, race or ethnicity when making voting recommendations.

Big investors have also backed off: BlackRock dropped a 30% target for diverse directors at S&P 500 companies in its portfolios. Fidelity, the mutual-fund giant, stopped threatening to vote against directors on insufficiently diverse boards. Goldman, JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley all dropped similar requirements, too.

Goldman said its proxy-voting changes reflect shifts in the legal environment. "It doesn't change our strong belief that successful boards benefit from diverse backgrounds and perspectives," a spokeswoman said.

Then there are the activists. Anti-DEI groups began filing more shareholder proposals opposing diversity policies and other environmental, social and corporate-governance practices they describe as misguided or too liberal, reaching 123 proposals last year, according to ISS-Corporate, a unit of ISS.

Most shareholder proposals fail, and those opposing ESG policies rarely garner support from more than a few percent of shares cast. Like other activists, however, their proponents sometimes win concessions in return for dropping their proposals.

The activists have influential allies, too. President Trump started his term ordering federal agencies to end diversity-related contracts and programs, among other initiatives. Late last month, the federal General Services Administration proposed a new certification for organizations receiving federal grants — promising their practices don't violate federal antidiscrimination laws. They include "those labeled as Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion" programs, the GSA said.

Write to Theo Francis at theo.francis@wsj.com

data.title: TSX Tests New Highs
data.description:
The S&P/TSX Composite rose around 0.2% to trade above the 33,650 mark for the first time on Friday, positioning the index for a weekly gain of over 1.7% as surging gold prices and upbeat mining results offset pressure in the energy sector.

Materials outperformed after gold reclaimed the 5,000 level on safe haven demand fueled by escalating US-Iran tensions.

Agnico Eagle Mines gained 0.8% and Barrick Gold added 0.5% while Pan American Silver jumped over 2%.

In corporate news Lundin Mining climbed 4.5% after a profit beat while Fairfax Financial surged over 2.6%.

Financials traded with a positive bias despite a mixed macro backdrop where soft US GDP was countered by sticky core PCE inflation, with TD Bank and CIBC edging up 0.4%.

Conversely energy stocks including Suncor and Imperial Oil retreated as oil prices pared some weekly gains despite a 9 million barrel drop in US crude inventories.

The benchmark remains on track for its best week since January.
